segments.text: Part 6D: Carbohydrate metabolism overview Dr. Rizk

The Sun is Energy for Life
Phototrophs use light to drive synthesis of organic molecules
Heterotrophs use these as building blocks
CO 2 ,  O 2 , and H 2 O are recycled
Figure 17.1  The flow of energy in the biosphere is coupled to the carbon and oxygen cycles

17.2 What Can Be Learned From Metabolic Maps?
Metabolism consists of catabolism and anabolism
Catabolism: degradative pathways
Usually energy-yielding
Anabolism: biosynthetic pathways
Usually energy-requiring
Metabolic maps portray the principal reactions of intermediary metabolism

17.2 What Can Be Learned From Metabolic Maps?
Figure 17.2 A metabolic map, indicating the reactions of intermediary metabolism and the enzymes that catalyze them.  More than 500 different chemical intermediates, or metabolites, and a greater number of enzymes are represented here.

17.2 What Can Be Learned From Metabolic Maps?
Figure 17.3 The metabolic map as a set of dots and lines.  The heavy dots and lines trace the central energy-releasing pathways known as glycolysis and the citric acid cycle.

Organization in Pathways
Pathways consist of sequential steps
The enzymes may be separate
Or may form a multienzyme complex
Or may be a membrane-bound system
New research indicates that multienzyme complexes are more common than once thought

Figure 17.6  Anabolism and Catabolism are Interrelated
Products from one provide substrates for the other.
Many intermediates are shared between anabolism and catabolism.

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Endergonic:
Free energy absorbed during the process
Free energy: (ΔG) is positive
Exergonic:
Free energy released during the process
Free energy: (ΔG) is negative
ΔG = -RT ln K eq

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Endergonic:
Process is non-spontaneous
Exergonic:
Process is spontaneous
The more negative the free energy, the more spontaneous the process will be.

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Question:
Reaction L has a ΔG of -2.5 kcal/ mol , whereas reaction M has a ΔG of +2.5 kcal/mol.
L is spontaneous, energy is absorbed
M is spontaneous, energy is released
L is non-spontaneous, energy is released
M is spontaneous, energy is absorbed
L is spontaneous, energy is released

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Question:
Reaction L has a ΔG of -5 kcal/ mol , whereas reaction M has a ΔG of -2.5 kcal/mol. Which reaction will have the faster rate?
There is not enough information to know about the rate. ΔG does not tell you anything about the reaction rate

Endothermic vs. Exothermic
Endothermic:
Heat energy absorbed during the process
Enthalpy: (ΔH) is positive
Exothermic:
Heat energy released during the process
Enthalpy: (ΔH) is negative
ΔG = ΔH - TΔS
Are all exothermic processes exergonic?
No, must consider entropy

Enthalpy vs. Entropy
ΔH: the amount of heat added to a system at constant pressure
This is useful, since pressure changes are not common in biological systems
If heat is added, ΔH is positive, process is endothermic
Entropy (S): the number of degrees of freedom within a system
If the number of degrees of freedom increases, ΔS is positive, process: “ entropically favorable ”
entropy is constantly increasing in the universe

The Pathways of Catabolism Converge to a Few End Products
Figure 17.7 The three stages of catabolism. Stage 1 : Proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids are broken down into their component building blocks. Stage 2 : The building blocks are degraded into the common product, the acetyl groups of acetyl-CoA. Stage 3 : Catabolism converges to three principal end products: water, carbon dioxide, and ammonia.

ATP Serves in a Cellular Energy Cycle
ATP is the energy currency of cells
Phototrophs transform light energy into the chemical energy of ATP
In heterotrophs, catabolism produces ATP, which drives activities of cells
ATP cycle carries energy from photosynthesis or catabolism to the energy-requiring processes of cells
Non-spontaneous reactions (positive ΔG) can be carried out by coupling to ATP hydrolysis
ATP and Energy Production

ATP and Energy Production A. General Features of ATP Hydrolysis
Hydrolysis of ATP cleaves 1 phosphate group.
This forms ADP and phosphate ( HPO 4 2− ),
releasing 7.3 kcal/mol of energy.
ENERGY

ATP and Energy Production B. General Features of ATP Phosphorylation
Phosphorylation is the reverse reaction, where a phosphate group is added to ADP , forming ATP requiring 7.3 kcal/mol of energy.
ENERGY

ATP and Energy Production A. General Features of ATP Phosphorylation
Any process (walking, running, breathing) is fueled by the release of energy when ATP is hydrolyzed to ADP.
Energy is absorbed and stored in ATP when it is synthesized from ADP.

ATP and Energy Production A. General Features of ATP Phosphorylation
To produce energy, food must be “burned” = oxidized
Carbon containing molecules (sugars, Fats, proteins) can be completely oxidized over many steps
What is the most oxidized form of Carbon?
CO 2
Example:
Glucose (C 6 H 12 O 6 ) +  3 O 2  6 H 2 O + 6 CO 2

Coenzymes in Metabolism A. Coenzymes NAD + and NADH
NAD and FAD are “coenzymes” and electron carriers
When sugars, proteins and Fats are oxidized:
NAD is reduced to NADH, it gains electrons in the form of H
FAD is reduced to FADH2, it gains electrons in the form of H
Carbon containing molecules (sugars, Fats, proteins) can be completely oxidized over many steps

The Substrates of Catabolism Contain Relatively Reduced Forms of Carbon
Figure 17.10  Comparison of the state of reduction of carbon atoms in biomolecules.  Chains of –CH 2 - groups are the most energy-rich form of reduced carbon in the biosphere.  Carbon dioxide is the final product of catabolism and the most oxidized form of carbon in the biosphere.

Metabolism: What ’ s the point
Carbon-containing molecules are burned (oxidized) to produce electrons in the form of hydrogen
Electron carriers FAD and NAD are reduced to FADH2 and NADH, shuttle electrons to the ETC
NADH and FADH2 donate electrons to oxygen to make H 2 O
NAD and FAD are regenerated and ATP is made
Fats & Carbohydrates
NAD + FAD
NAD H + FAD H 2
CO 2

NAD + Collects Electrons Released in Catabolism
Figure 17.11 Hydrogen and electrons released in the course of oxidative catabolism are transferred as hydride ions (H: - ) to the pyridine nucleotide, NAD + , to form NADH + H + in dehydrogenase reactions.

Coenzymes in Metabolism A. Coenzymes NAD + and NADH
NAD is a coenzyme acting as an oxidizing agent causes an oxidation reaction to occur, so the coenzyme is reduced .
NADH is a coenzyme acting as a reducing agent causes a reduction reaction to occur, so the coenzyme is oxidized
When a coenzyme acts as an oxidizing agent, it gains e − in the form of Hydrogen (NAD  NADH )
When a coenzyme acts as a reducing agent, it loses e − in the form of Hydrogen ( NADH  NAD)

NADPH Provides the Reducing Power for Anabolic Processes
Whereas catabolism is oxidative, anabolism is reductive
Biosynthesis typically relies on reducing equivalents  from NADPH
NADPH can be viewed as the carrier of electrons from catabolic reactions to anabolic reactions
In photosynthesis, light energy is used to pull electrons from water and transfer them to NADP +
O 2 is a by-product of this process

NADPH Provides the Reducing Power for Anabolic Processes
Figure 17.12 Transfer of reducing equivalents from catabolism to anabolism via the NADPH cycle.
A Summary of Vitamins and Coenzymes Discussed Elsewhere in the Text

Review question
All of the following processes take place in the mitochondria, EXCEPT :
Citric Acid cycle
Glycolysis
Oxidative phosphorylation
Fatty Acid Oxidation (degradation)
The Urea Cycle (nitrogen excretion)
Metabolic Pathways are Compartmentalized Within Cells

Locations of biochemical processes in the cell
The location in the cell | The biochemical process
Cytosol | Glycolysis, pentose phosphate shunt, FA synthesis and glycogen synthesis
Mitochondrial Matrix | β-oxidation of FAs and TCA cycle
Inner mitochondrial membrane | Oxidative phosphorylation
Both cytosol and mitochondria | Gluconeogenesis, urea cycle and heme synthesis

17.6  What Food Substances Form the Basis of Human Nutrition?
Protein is a rich source of nitrogen and also provides essential amino acids
Carbohydrates provide needed energy and essential components for nucleotides and nucleic acids
Lipids provide essential fatty acids that are key components of membranes and also important signal molecules
Fiber – whether soluble or insoluble – can be a beneficial component in the human diet

An Overview of Metabolism
Carbohydrates are hydrolyzed into monosaccharides beginning with amylase enzymes in saliva and continuing in the small intestine.

An Overview of Metabolism A. Stage [1]—Digestion
Protein digestion begins when stomach acid denatures the protein and pepsin begins to cleave the large protein backbone into smaller peptides.
Then, in the small intestines, trypsin and chymotrypsin cleave the peptides into amino acids.
2X

An Overview of Metabolism B. Stage [2]—Formation of Acetyl CoA
The majority of what we eat is (eventually) converted into Acetyl CoA

An Overview of Metabolism
The citric acid cycle converts Acetyl CoA to CO 2
Produces ATP and NADH and FADH2
CO 2
The citric acid cycle takes place in the mitochondria
NAD +
NADH
FAD
FADH2

Citric Acid Cycle
The citric acid cycle is where CO 2 is produced
CO 2

FADH2
NAD is converted to NADH
FAD is converted to FADH2
Some ATP is produced
What happens to the electrons carried by NADH and FADH2 produced from the citric acid cycle?
Final stage: the electron transport chain

An Overview of Metabolism D. The Electron Transport Chain
This process is called “ oxidative phosphorylation ” :
Oxygen is accepts electrons from NADH and FADH2, converting them back to NAD and FAD
When oxygen gains e- in the form of hydrogen, water is released
Phosphorylation: ADP + Pi  ATP
